title: CSR: Phone Skills & Telephone Etiquette
description: A comprehensive BLUEEE CSR course on professional veterinary phone communication, including greetings, information gathering, holds, triage awareness, callbacks, documentation, de-escalation, and busy-front-desk prioritization.

modules.title: Module 1 — First Impressions on the Phone

lessons.title: The Greeting, the Tone, and the First 20 Seconds

blocks.prompt: A new client calls for the first time and sounds nervous. What should the CSR do first?

options.text: Answer with “Hello?” and wait for the client to figure out who they reached.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Use a complete, warm greeting with the clinic name, your name, and an invitation to help.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Answer with “hold please” before knowing whether the call is urgent.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: The phone is often a client’s first real interaction with the hospital. A professional greeting should identify the clinic, introduce the CSR, and invite the client to explain the reason for the call. The tone should sound calm, organized, and kind even when the desk is busy. This first moment teaches the client whether the clinic is prepared to help them.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What does a strong phone greeting accomplish?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: It identifies the clinic, introduces the CSR, and starts the call with calm professionalism.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.front: Four parts of a professional phone greeting
blocks.back: Greeting + clinic name + your name + invitation to help.

blocks.content: Future media: welcoming veterinary front-desk hero image; Unsplash keywords: veterinary receptionist, animal hospital front desk, veterinary team.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

lessons.title: Gather Details Without Sounding Like an Interrogation

blocks.prompt: A client says, “I just need to talk to the doctor.” What is the best response?

options.text: Say “the doctor is busy” and end the call.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Acknowledge the request and gather patient name, callback number, and the concern so the message is useful.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Demand a full medical history and then decide if the doctor should call.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: The CSR should gather enough information to route the call correctly: client name, patient name, callback number, reason for call, urgency, and who should receive the message. Good questions protect the medical team’s time and prevent repeated callbacks. The CSR should write the client’s concern in clear, factual language without diagnosing.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What makes a phone message useful?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: It has the right patient/client details, callback number, concern, urgency, routing destination, and factual wording.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Enough detail for the next person to act without starting over.

blocks.content: Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

modules.title: Module 3 — Holds, Transfers, and Multiple Lines

lessons.title: Managing Calls Without Making Clients Feel Forgotten

blocks.prompt: You are on a routine scheduling call when another line rings. What is best?

options.text: Ignore the second line until it stops ringing.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Ask permission to briefly hold, answer the second line to assess urgency, then manage the calls with clear updates.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Hang up on the first caller to answer the second.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Busy clinics may have several calls, walk-ins, and checkouts happening at once. The CSR should ask permission before placing a caller on hold, thank them when returning, and check back if a hold is long. Transfers should include a clear explanation of what is happening and a backup plan if the person is unavailable.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Ask permission, wait for the answer, return promptly, and thank the client for holding.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: “May I place you on a brief hold?”

blocks.content: Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

modules.title: Module 4 — Urgency Recognition on the Phone

lessons.title: Emergency Clues a CSR Should Never Ignore

blocks.prompt: A client says their male cat keeps trying to urinate and nothing is coming out. What should happen?

options.text: Tell them it is probably constipation and to monitor.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Treat it as urgent, gather key details quickly, and alert the medical team per protocol.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Schedule a routine appointment next week.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: The CSR does not diagnose but must recognize red flags such as difficulty breathing, collapse, uncontrolled bleeding, seizures, toxin ingestion, possible bloat, and inability to urinate. These calls should be routed immediately according to clinic protocol. The CSR should stay calm and avoid giving medical advice.
Never reassure a client that a red-flag symptom is “probably fine.”

blocks.question: What is the CSR role in triage calls?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Recognize red flags, gather essentials, and route quickly without diagnosing.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

lessons.title: When the Client Wants Answers Now

blocks.prompt: A client says, “Nobody called me with results. Is my dog okay?”

options.text: Say “If nobody called, it must be fine.”
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Validate the worry, check status, and route/document the message for the appropriate medical team member.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Say “the doctor probably forgot.”
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Results and callback calls often come from worried clients. The CSR should validate the concern, check the chart or communication log if allowed, explain the next step, and document the call. Avoid guessing, interpreting results, blaming the doctor, or promising exact callback times unless confirmed.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: Which response stays inside CSR scope?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Explain the communication process and route the request without interpreting medical results.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Check status, route accurately, document, and do not interpret results.

blocks.content: Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.
